label: ai
topic: Finding confidence through an unexpected extracurricular activity
text:
For most of my life, I was the kind of person other people described using the word "quiet." Not shy, exactly — shyness implies a desire to connect that is thwarted by fear. I was something more settled than that. I had accepted, with the calm pragmatism of someone who has been picked last for enough teams, that I was not someone who took up space.

So when my friend Marcus told me he had signed me up for the school radio station, I assumed he was joking. Marcus had been doing the morning announcements since sophomore year, his voice booming through the hallways at seven-forty-five with a confidence I found both admirable and baffling.

"We need someone for the Thursday afternoon slot," he said. "Just an hour. Play some music, talk a little."

"I don't talk," I said. "That's my whole thing."

"You talk to me. You're actually pretty funny when you talk to me."

This was technically true, but talking to Marcus in the cafeteria and talking into a microphone that broadcast to the entire school were separated by approximately the same distance as wading in a kiddie pool and crossing the English Channel.

I said yes because Marcus had once lent me his notes for an entire semester of AP History without being asked, and I felt I owed him something roughly equivalent in personal discomfort.

The radio booth was a converted supply closet on the second floor, barely large enough for two chairs and a desk covered in equipment that looked like it had been purchased during the Clinton administration. The microphone was a silver thing on a black stand, slightly dented, with a foam cover someone had drawn a smiley face on in Sharpie.

My first broadcast was terrible. I played four songs, mispronounced an artist's name, and filled the gaps between tracks with silence so dense you could have used it as insulation. At one point I said "So, um, that was that song" and then could not think of a single additional word.

But here is the thing about a supply closet with a microphone: nobody can see you. There is no audience of faces to read, no body language to interpret. There is only your voice and the strange, private intimacy of speaking to people who are choosing to listen.

By the third week, I started talking between songs. Small things at first — a comment about the weather, a book recommendation, a gentle complaint about the vending machine that accepted your money and offered you nothing in return. By the fifth week, I was doing bits. I had a recurring segment called "Parking Lot Update" that was entirely fictional and involved an escalating feud between two imaginary drivers over a contested space near the gymnasium.

People started mentioning it. A girl from my chemistry class told me that Parking Lot Update was the funniest thing on the radio, and since it was the only thing on the radio attempting to be funny, this was both a compliment and a statement of statistical fact.

What the radio taught me was not how to be loud. It taught me that there is a version of confidence that does not require volume or visibility. That being quiet is not the same as having nothing to say — sometimes it just means you haven't found the room where your frequency is the one people are tuned to.

Senior year, I hosted the Thursday slot and the Tuesday morning announcements. I still wouldn't call myself an extrovert. But I no longer describe myself as someone who does not talk. I am someone who talks on Tuesdays and Thursdays, from a supply closet on the second floor, into a dented microphone with a smiley face drawn on it.

That turns out to be enough.
